key_insight: Raising argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produces denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with roughly twice the thickness and about half the porosity, while also improving friction performance.
engineering_relevance: Argon flow is a practical process lever for tuning coating density, wear resistance, and failure stability in self-fluxing thermal spray coatings.
method: Air plasma sprayed NiCrFeBSiC self-fluxing metal-ceramic coatings
Compared argon flow rates of 1600 and 1800 L/h
Evaluated microstructure, microhardness, elastic modulus, tribology, and scratch adhesion
evidence_signal: The abstract reports microhardness of 1015.8 ± 81.5 HV, elastic modulus of 203.9 ± 5.4 GPa, and a lower friction coefficient of about 0.107 at 1800 L/h, with similar critical scratch loads but more stable failure behavior.
watch_item: The study compares only two argon flow settings, so broader process optimization and scalability remain uncertain.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
